news_summary: Scientists have uncovered ancient supernova debris on Earth, discovered a primitive galaxy from the early universe, and NASA’s TESS mission continues to reveal stunning cosmic phenomena, while new research offers fresh insights into the history of Halley’s comet.
news_summary_fr: Des scientifiques ont mis au jour sur Terre des débris d'une supernova ancienne, ont découvert une galaxie primitive datant des débuts de l'univers, et la mission TESS de la NASA continue de révéler des phénomènes cosmiques époustouflants, tandis que de nouvelles recherches apportent un éclairage inédit sur l'histoire de la comète de Halley.
news_summary_es: Los científicos han hallado restos de una supernova antigua en la Tierra, han descubierto una galaxia primitiva del universo primitivo y la misión TESS de la NASA sigue revelando impresionantes fenómenos cósmicos, mientras que nuevas investigaciones aportan nuevos datos sobre la historia del cometa Halley.

articles.title: Earth is flying through ancient supernova debris and scientists found the evidence in Antarctic ice
articles.summary: Earth is quietly collecting radioactive debris from an ancient stellar explosion as our Solar System drifts through a giant cloud of gas and dust between the stars. Scientists analyzing Antarctic ice up to 80,000 years old discovered traces of iron-60 — a rare isotope forged in supernova explosions — and found evidence that this “cosmic ash” has been lingering inside the Local Interstellar Cloud for ages. The discovery suggests the cloud surrounding our Solar System was shaped by a long-ago exploding star, offering researchers a new way to study our galactic neighborhood.
articles.content:
Scientists have uncovered new evidence that Earth is traveling through a cloud of ancient stellar debris left behind by a long ago exploding star. By studying Antarctic ice that formed tens of thousands of years ago, researchers detected traces of iron-60, a rare radioactive form of iron created during supernova explosions. The findings support the idea that the Local Interstellar Cloud surrounding our Solar System contains material from an ancient stellar blast.

The research was led by an international team from the Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf (HZDR), and the results were published in Physical Review Letters.

Rare Supernova Iron Found in Antarctic Ice

Iron-60 forms deep inside massive stars and is released into space when those stars explode as supernovae. Previous studies found evidence that Earth was exposed to iron-60 from nearby supernova explosions millions of years ago. However, no recent stellar explosions close to Earth are known, leaving scientists uncertain about the source of iron-60 discovered in relatively young Antarctic snow a few years ago.

"Our idea was that the Local Interstellar Cloud contains iron-60 and can store it over long time periods. As the Solar System moves through the cloud, Earth could collect this material. However, we couldn't prove this at the time," explains Dr. Dominik Koll from the Institute of Ion Beam Physics and Materials Research at HZDR.

To investigate further, Koll and Prof. Anton Wallner studied additional geological samples in recent years, including deep sea sediments up to 30,000 years old. Those samples also contained iron-60, but scientists still could not fully rule out other explanations.

The newly analyzed Antarctic ice samples are much older, dating back between 40,000 and 80,000 years. According to the team, the results strongly point to the Local Interstellar Cloud as the source of the radioactive material.

"This means that the clouds surrounding the Solar System are linked to a stellar explosion. And for the first time, this gives us the opportunity to investigate the origin of these clouds," says Koll.

Solar System Traveling Through an Interstellar Cloud

Scientists believe the Solar System entered the Local Interstellar Cloud several tens of thousands of years ago and is expected to exit it again within the next few thousand years. Researchers say the Solar System is currently located near the cloud's outer edge.

For the study, the team focused on an ice core covering the time period when the Solar System may have entered the cloud. The sample was provided by the Alfred Wegener Institute Helmholtz Centre for Polar and Marine Research (AWI) through the European EPICA ice drilling project.

When researchers compared the ice core results with earlier snow and deep sea sediment measurements, they discovered that less iron-60 reached Earth between 40,000 and 80,000 years ago than today.

"This suggests that we were previously in a medium with lower iron-60 content, or that the cloud itself exhibits strong density variations," explains Koll.

The team found that the iron-60 signal changes significantly over periods of only tens of thousands of years, which is relatively rapid on cosmic timescales. This helped researchers rule out competing theories, including the idea that the material came from older supernova explosions that slowly faded over millions of years.

Extracting Tiny Traces of Iron-60

To carry out the study, researchers transported about 300 kilograms of Antarctic ice from AWI in Bremerhaven to Dresden for chemical processing. After extensive preparation, only a few hundred milligrams of dust remained.

Scientists then carefully separated the iron-60 while trying to avoid losing any material during the process.

At the DREsden Accelerator Mass Spectrometry (DREAMS) laboratory at HZDR, the team tested the prepared samples using two additional radioactive isotopes, beryllium-10 and aluminium-26. Because the expected levels of these isotopes in Antarctic ice are already well understood, researchers could verify that no iron-60 had been lost during preparation.

Detecting a Handful of Atoms

For the final measurements, scientists used the Heavy Ion Accelerator Facility (HIAF) at the Australian National University, which is currently the world's only facility capable of detecting such extremely small quantities of iron-60. Using electric and magnetic filters, the machine separated atoms by mass until only a few iron-60 atoms remained from an original sample containing 10 trillion atoms.

"It's like searching for a needle in 50,000 football stadiums filled to the roof with hay. The machine finds the needle in an hour," explains Annabel Rolofs from the University of Bonn.

"Through many years of collaboration with international colleagues, we have developed an extremely sensitive method that now allows us to detect the clear signature of cosmic explosions that occurred millions of years ago in geological archives today," summarizes Wallner.

Researchers are now planning to study even older ice cores from a time before the Solar System entered the Local Interstellar Cloud. The AWI is participating in the Beyond EPICA - Oldest Ice project, which aims to recover ice samples dating back even farther into Earth's past.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery that Earth is currently traveling through debris from an ancient supernova, evidenced by radioactive isotopes found in Antarctic ice. This finding provides new insights into our galactic environment and the history of the Local Interstellar Cloud, representing a meaningful advancement in space science with broad public interest and substantial scientific context.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find ancient supernova debris in Antarctic ice as Earth moves through cosmic cloud
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Our Solar System is moving through a cloud of gas and dust in space called the Local Interstellar Cloud. Scientists have wondered what this cloud is made of and where it came from.

**What happened** – Researchers studied Antarctic ice up to 80,000 years old and found tiny amounts of iron-60, a rare radioactive element made in supernova explosions. This discovery shows that the Local Interstellar Cloud contains debris from a star that exploded long ago. The study was led by scientists from the Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf and involved analyzing ice cores and deep sea sediments.

**Impact** – Finding iron-60 in ancient ice proves that the cloud around our Solar System carries the remains of a supernova. This is important because it gives scientists a new way to learn about the history and environment of our cosmic neighborhood. It also helps explain how material from distant stars can reach Earth.

**What’s next step** – Researchers plan to study even older ice samples to learn more about the time before our Solar System entered this cloud. Projects like Beyond EPICA aim to collect ice that is older than 80,000 years, which may reveal more about the stars and clouds near us in space.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have found clear signs of ancient star explosions in Antarctic ice, showing that Earth is traveling through a cloud of supernova debris.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des débris d’une supernova ancienne découverts dans la glace antarctique alors que la Terre traverse un nuage cosmique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Notre Système solaire se déplace à travers un nuage de gaz et de poussières dans l’espace appelé le Nuage Interstellaire Local. Les scientifiques s’interrogeaient sur sa composition et son origine.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont analysé de la glace antarctique vieille de jusqu’à 80 000 ans et ont trouvé des traces d’un élément radioactif rare, le fer-60, créé lors d’explosions d’étoiles appelées supernovas. Cette découverte montre que le Nuage Interstellaire Local contient des débris d’une étoile ayant explosé il y a très longtemps. L’étude a été menée par des scientifiques du Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf et a utilisé des carottes de glace et des sédiments marins.

**Impact** – La présence de fer-60 dans la glace ancienne prouve que le nuage autour de notre Système solaire porte les restes d’une supernova. Cela offre une nouvelle manière d’étudier l’histoire et l’environnement de notre voisinage galactique. Cela explique aussi comment des matériaux d’étoiles lointaines peuvent atteindre la Terre.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d’étudier des échantillons de glace encore plus vieux pour mieux comprendre la période avant que notre Système solaire ne pénètre ce nuage. Des projets comme Beyond EPICA visent à récupérer de la glace plus ancienne, ce qui pourrait révéler davantage sur les étoiles et les nuages proches de nous dans l’espace.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont trouvé des preuves claires d’explosions d’étoiles anciennes dans la glace antarctique, montrant que la Terre traverse un nuage de débris de supernova.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos encuentran restos de una supernova antigua en hielo antártico mientras la Tierra atraviesa una nube cósmica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Nuestro Sistema Solar se mueve a través de una nube de gas y polvo en el espacio llamada la Nube Interestelar Local. Los científicos querían saber de qué está hecha y cómo se formó.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores estudiaron hielo antártico de hasta 80,000 años de antigüedad y encontraron pequeñas cantidades de hierro-60, un elemento radiactivo raro creado en explosiones de estrellas llamadas supernovas. Este hallazgo muestra que la Nube Interestelar Local contiene restos de una estrella que explotó hace mucho tiempo. El estudio fue dirigido por científicos del Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf y usó núcleos de hielo y sedimentos marinos.

**Impacto** – Encontrar hierro-60 en hielo antiguo demuestra que la nube alrededor de nuestro Sistema Solar lleva restos de una supernova. Esto es importante porque ofrece una nueva forma de entender la historia y el entorno de nuestro vecindario cósmico. También ayuda a explicar cómo materiales de estrellas lejanas pueden llegar a la Tierra.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean estudiar muestras de hielo aún más antiguas para aprender más sobre el tiempo antes de que nuestro Sistema Solar entrara en esta nube. Proyectos como Beyond EPICA buscan recolectar hielo más viejo, lo que podría revelar más sobre las estrellas y nubes cercanas a nosotros en el espacio.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos encontraron señales claras de explosiones estelares antiguas en hielo antártico, mostrando que la Tierra viaja a través de una nube de restos de supernova.
articles.image_prompt: A serene Antarctic ice landscape under a softly glowing starry sky, with delicate sparkling dust particles symbolizing rare iron-60 gently drifting through the air, while a subtle, translucent cloud of glowing cosmic debris envelops the distant Solar System silhouette near the horizon.

articles.title: An ultra-faint, chemically primitive galaxy forming in the reionization era
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 13 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10374-1LAP1-B—an ultra-faint and tiny galaxy that formed in the reionization era and is strongly magnified by gravitational lensing—is chemically primitive and hosts very few stars in an otherwise dominant dark matter halo.
articles.content:

Jenkins, E. B. A unified representation of gas-phase element depletions in the interstellar medium. Astrophys. J. 700, 1299–1348 (2009).

van Hoof, P. A. M., Weingartner, J. C., Martin, P. G., Volk, K. & Ferland, G. J. Grain size distributions and photoelectric heating in ionized media. Mon. Not. R. Astron. Soc. 350, 1330–1341 (2004).

Nakajima, K. Reduced JWST/NIRSpec spectrum for ‘An ultra-faint, chemically primitive galaxy forming in the reionization era’. Zenodo https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18831461 (2026).

articles.reasoning: The article reports the discovery and analysis of an ultra-faint, chemically primitive galaxy from the reionization era, providing significant insights into early cosmic history and galaxy formation. This discovery has broad scientific significance for understanding the early universe and the formation of the first stars and galaxies, supported by detailed spectroscopic data and gravitational lensing observations. The story is focused, substantive, and represents a meaningful advancement in space and cosmology research.
articles.personality_title: Discovery of a tiny, ancient galaxy sheds light on the early universe
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study the early universe to understand how the first stars and galaxies formed. The reionization era, which happened about 13 billion years ago, marks an important time when the universe changed from dark and foggy to clear and bright.

**What happened** – Researchers discovered a very faint and small galaxy called LAP1-B that formed during the reionization era. This galaxy is chemically primitive, meaning it has very few heavy elements, and contains only a small number of stars. The galaxy’s light is made easier to see because of a natural magnifying effect called gravitational lensing, where a massive object bends and brightens the light from distant objects behind it.

**Impact** – Finding such a faint and simple galaxy helps scientists learn about the first stages of galaxy formation and the chemical makeup of the early universe. LAP1-B is important because it shows what galaxies looked like before many stars had formed and before heavy elements were common. This discovery helps fill in gaps about how dark matter and gas came together to form the first galaxies.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue using powerful telescopes, like the James Webb Space Telescope, to study galaxies like LAP1-B in more detail. They hope to find more ultra-faint galaxies and understand how they grew and changed over time. This research can also guide future studies about the first stars and black holes.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The discovery of LAP1-B, a tiny and chemically simple galaxy from the universe’s early days, offers a new window into how the first galaxies formed and evolved.
articles.personality_title_fr: Découverte d'une minuscule galaxie ancienne éclaire l'univers primitif
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient l'univers primitif pour comprendre comment les premières étoiles et galaxies se sont formées. L’ère de la réionisation, il y a environ 13 milliards d’années, est une période clé où l'univers est passé d'un état sombre et brumeux à un univers clair et lumineux.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont découvert une galaxie très faible et petite appelée LAP1-B, formée durant l’ère de la réionisation. Cette galaxie est chimiquement primitive, ce qui signifie qu'elle contient très peu d'éléments lourds, et possède seulement un petit nombre d’étoiles. Sa lumière est rendue plus visible grâce à un effet naturel appelé lentille gravitationnelle, où un objet massif courbe et amplifie la lumière des objets lointains derrière lui.

**Impact** – Trouver une galaxie aussi faible et simple aide les scientifiques à mieux comprendre les premières étapes de la formation des galaxies et la composition chimique de l'univers ancien. LAP1-B est importante car elle montre à quoi ressemblaient les galaxies avant la formation de nombreuses étoiles et la présence d’éléments lourds. Cette découverte complète les connaissances sur la manière dont la matière noire et le gaz se sont assemblés pour créer les premières galaxies.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront à utiliser des télescopes puissants, comme le télescope spatial James Webb, pour étudier plus en détail des galaxies comme LAP1-B. Ils espèrent découvrir d’autres galaxies ultra-faibles et comprendre comment elles ont grandi et évolué avec le temps. Ces recherches guideront aussi les futures études sur les premières étoiles et trous noirs.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La découverte de LAP1-B, une galaxie minuscule et chimiquement simple des débuts de l’univers, offre une nouvelle fenêtre sur la formation et l’évolution des premières galaxies.
articles.personality_title_es: Descubrimiento de una pequeña galaxia antigua ilumina el universo temprano
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian el universo temprano para entender cómo se formaron las primeras estrellas y galaxias. La era de la reionización, ocurrida hace unos 13 mil millones de años, es un momento clave cuando el universo pasó de estar oscuro y nublado a ser claro y brillante.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores descubrieron una galaxia muy débil y pequeña llamada LAP1-B que se formó durante la era de la reionización. Esta galaxia es químicamente primitiva, lo que significa que tiene muy pocos elementos pesados y contiene solo unas pocas estrellas. La luz de esta galaxia es más fácil de ver gracias a un efecto natural llamado lente gravitacional, donde un objeto muy masivo dobla y amplifica la luz de objetos distantes detrás de él.

**Impacto** – Encontrar una galaxia tan débil y simple ayuda a los científicos a aprender sobre las primeras etapas de la formación de galaxias y la composición química del universo temprano. LAP1-B es importante porque muestra cómo eran las galaxias antes de que se formaran muchas estrellas y antes de que los elementos pesados fueran comunes. Este descubrimiento ayuda a completar la historia de cómo la materia oscura y el gas se unieron para formar las primeras galaxias.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán usando telescopios poderosos, como el Telescopio Espacial James Webb, para estudiar galaxias como LAP1-B con más detalle. Esperan encontrar más galaxias ultra débiles y entender cómo crecieron y cambiaron con el tiempo. Esta investigación también ayudará en futuros estudios sobre las primeras estrellas y agujeros negros.

**Frase clave** – El descubrimiento de LAP1-B, una galaxia pequeña y químicamente simple de los primeros días del universo, ofrece una nueva mirada sobre cómo se formaron y evolucionaron las primeras galaxias.
articles.image_prompt: A delicate, glowing cluster of tiny, faint galaxies made of soft, pale blue and silver light, surrounded by swirling clouds of pristine hydrogen and helium gas under a vast, dark cosmic backdrop, symbolizing the birth of chemically primitive galaxies in the early universe’s reionization era.

articles.title: NASA’s Planet-Hunting TESS Reveals Dazzling Night Sky
articles.summary: NASA’s TESS has released its most complete view of the starry sky to date
articles.content:
NASA’s TESS (Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite) has released its most complete view of the starry sky to date, filling in gaps from previous observations. Nearly 6,000 colored dots scattered across the image show the locations of either confirmed or candidate exoplanets — worlds beyond our solar system — identified by the mission as of September 2025 at the end of TESS’s second extended mission.

“Over the last eight years, TESS has become a fire hose of exoplanet science,” said Rebekah Hounsell, a TESS associate project scientist at the University of Maryland Baltimore County and NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center in Greenbelt, Maryland. “It’s helped us find planets of all different sizes, from tiny Mercury-like ones to those larger than Jupiter. Some of them are even in the habitable zone, where liquid water might be possible on the surface, an important factor in our search for life beyond Earth.”

The TESS mission scans a wide swath of the sky, called a sector, for about a month at a time using its four cameras. These long stares allow the spacecraft to track the brightness changes of tens of thousands of stars, looking for variations in their light that might come from orbiting planets.

Researchers assembled an all-sky mosaic made of 96 sectors observed between April 2018, when TESS began its work, and September 2025.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific achievement by NASA's TESS mission, providing the most complete view of the starry sky with nearly 6,000 confirmed or candidate exoplanets identified. This discovery has broad significance for humanity's understanding of the universe and the search for life beyond Earth, representing a major breakthrough in space exploration and astronomy.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s TESS mission maps nearly 6,000 distant planets in a new sky view
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA’s Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite (TESS) has been exploring the sky since 2018. Its goal is to find planets outside our solar system, called exoplanets, by watching stars for tiny changes in brightness.

**What happened** – In September 2025, TESS released the most complete picture of the night sky it has ever made. This image combines data from 96 sections of the sky observed over eight years. It shows nearly 6,000 spots that mark confirmed or possible exoplanets.

**Impact** – This new sky map is special because it fills in previous gaps and gives scientists a clearer picture of where planets are beyond our solar system. TESS has found planets of many sizes, including some that might have temperatures suitable for liquid water, an important factor for life.

**What's next step** – Scientists will use this detailed map to study these planets more closely. Future missions might focus on the most interesting ones, especially those in the so-called "habitable zone" where life could exist.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s TESS mission has created the most detailed map yet of thousands of planets around other stars, helping us learn more about worlds beyond our own.
articles.personality_title_fr: La mission TESS de la NASA cartographie près de 6 000 planètes lointaines dans une nouvelle vue du ciel
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le satellite d’enquête d’exoplanètes en transit (TESS) de la NASA explore le ciel depuis 2018. Son objectif est de trouver des planètes en dehors de notre système solaire, appelées exoplanètes, en observant les étoiles pour détecter de petits changements de luminosité.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En septembre 2025, TESS a publié l’image la plus complète du ciel nocturne jamais réalisée. Cette image combine des données de 96 secteurs du ciel observés sur huit ans. Elle montre près de 6 000 points qui indiquent des exoplanètes confirmées ou possibles.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle carte du ciel est importante car elle comble des lacunes précédentes et offre aux scientifiques une meilleure vue des planètes au-delà de notre système solaire. TESS a trouvé des planètes de différentes tailles, y compris certaines qui pourraient avoir des températures permettant la présence d’eau liquide, un facteur clé pour la vie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques utiliseront cette carte détaillée pour étudier ces planètes de plus près. Les futures missions pourraient se concentrer sur les plus intéressantes, surtout celles situées dans la « zone habitable » où la vie pourrait exister.

**Phrase clé** – La mission TESS de la NASA a créé la carte la plus détaillée à ce jour de milliers de planètes autour d’autres étoiles, nous aidant à mieux comprendre les mondes au-delà du nôtre.
articles.personality_title_es: La misión TESS de la NASA mapea cerca de 6,000 planetas distantes en una nueva vista del cielo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El satélite de búsqueda de exoplanetas por tránsito (TESS) de la NASA ha estado explorando el cielo desde 2018. Su objetivo es encontrar planetas fuera de nuestro sistema solar, llamados exoplanetas, observando estrellas para detectar pequeños cambios en su brillo.

**Qué pasó** – En septiembre de 2025, TESS lanzó la imagen más completa del cielo nocturno que ha hecho hasta ahora. Esta imagen combina datos de 96 sectores del cielo observados durante ocho años. Muestra casi 6,000 puntos que señalan exoplanetas confirmados o posibles.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo mapa del cielo es especial porque llena espacios vacíos anteriores y da a los científicos una imagen más clara de dónde están los planetas fuera de nuestro sistema solar. TESS ha encontrado planetas de muchos tamaños, incluyendo algunos que podrían tener temperaturas adecuadas para agua líquida, un factor importante para la vida.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos usarán este mapa detallado para estudiar estos planetas más de cerca. Las futuras misiones podrían enfocarse en los más interesantes, especialmente en aquellos en la llamada "zona habitable" donde podría existir vida.

**Frase clave** – La misión TESS de la NASA ha creado el mapa más detallado hasta ahora de miles de planetas alrededor de otras estrellas, ayudándonos a aprender más sobre mundos más allá del nuestro.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vast, star-filled night sky mosaic composed of thousands of softly glowing colored dots representing distant exoplanets scattered across interconnected constellations, with a stylized telescope silhouette gently observing the scene beneath, all rendered in natural, muted blues, purples, and soft amber hues.

articles.title: Halley’s comet may be named after the wrong person
articles.summary: A medieval monk may have beaten Edmond Halley to one of astronomy’s greatest discoveries by nearly 700 years. Researchers say Eilmer of Malmesbury recognized that the blazing comet seen in 1066 was the same one he had witnessed in 989. At the time, comets were viewed as terrifying omens tied to war and royal deaths, adding even more drama to the famous celestial event shown in the Bayeux Tapestry. The discovery is sparking debate over whether Halley’s Comet deserves a different name.
articles.content:
Researchers say the famous comet known as Halley's Comet may have been identified as a repeating object centuries before British astronomer Edmond Halley gave it his name. According to new research involving Professor Simon Portegies Zwart, an English monk named Eilmer of Malmesbury appears to have recognized that the same comet returned to the skies decades apart during the 11th century.

The discovery comes from accounts written by the 12th century historian William of Malmesbury. Although scholars have long known about the writings, researchers say the significance of the descriptions was overlooked until now. Portegies Zwart and researcher Lewis argue that the monk, also known as Aethelmaer, witnessed the comet during two separate appearances and understood they were connected events.

Their work appears in the book 'Dorestad and Everything After. Ports, townscapes & travelers in Europe, 800-1100'.

Edmond Halley and the Famous Comet

Edmond Halley later became famous for identifying the periodic nature of the bright comet now officially called 1P/Halley. He concluded that the comets recorded in 1531, 1607, and 1682 were actually the same object returning roughly every 76 years.

That discovery eventually led to the comet being named after him.

Halley's Comet Over Medieval Europe

The comet's appearance in 1066 drew attention across much of the world. Historical records show it was observed in China for more than two months. Although the comet reached peak brightness on April 22, 1066, it was not visible in Brittany and the British Isles until April 24.

The comet became one of the most famous symbols associated with the year 1066 and even appears in the Bayeux Tapestry, the medieval artwork depicting the Norman conquest of England.

Medieval Fears and Royal Omens

Halley's Comet crossed the skies during the short reign of King Harold Godwinson, who ruled England from January 6 to October 14, 1066. Research by Portegies Zwart and Lewis found references to comet sightings on five occasions during the surrounding centuries.

At the time, comets were widely viewed as warnings of disaster. Oral traditions linked them to famine, war, and the deaths of kings throughout the British Isles. The researchers also point to reports of another comet connected to the death of Archbishop Sigeric of Canterbury in 995, even though no such comet appears in surviving chronicles. They suggest this may represent a medieval form of "fake news," or perhaps an exaggerated story intended to frighten people with warnings of divine punishment.

By 1066, Eilmer (or Aethelmaer) of Malmesbury was likely already an old man. When he saw the comet return, he reportedly realized he had witnessed the same object decades earlier in 989. As was common during the medieval period, the king was warned that the comet signaled approaching catastrophe.

Calls To Reconsider the Comet's Name

The researchers believe the comet's history raises questions about whether it should continue carrying Halley's name, since earlier observers may already have recognized its repeated appearances centuries before Halley's work.

Portegies Zwart said: "This research was great fun to do, but I also found it challenging to work within such an interdisciplinary project alongside a historian. Nevertheless, we plan to carry out further research into this kind of periodic comet."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant historical and scientific discovery that challenges long-held beliefs about the identification of Halley's Comet, highlighting earlier recognition by a medieval monk. This broadens our understanding of astronomy history and enriches cultural knowledge, inspiring curiosity and appreciation for interdisciplinary research.
articles.personality_title: Medieval monk may have recognized Halley's Comet centuries before Edmond Halley
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Halley's Comet is a famous comet that comes close to Earth about every 76 years. It is named after Edmond Halley, an astronomer who in the 1700s showed that the comet seen in several years was actually the same one returning. 

**What happened** – New research shows that a medieval English monk named Eilmer of Malmesbury may have noticed the comet's repeated appearances almost 700 years before Halley. Eilmer saw the comet in 989 and again in 1066, and he seemed to realize it was the same object. This discovery comes from old writings by historian William of Malmesbury, which were studied by modern researchers.

**Impact** – This finding changes how we understand the history of astronomy. It suggests that people in the Middle Ages recognized the comet's pattern long before modern science confirmed it. It also shows how medieval people reacted to comets, often seeing them as signs of important or scary events, like wars or the deaths of kings. The story even connects to famous artwork, like the Bayeux Tapestry, which shows the comet in 1066.

**What's next step** – The researchers plan to continue studying historical records to learn more about early observations of comets. Their work may lead to new ideas about how people in the past understood the sky and could even spark discussions about whether the comet's name should be changed to honor earlier observers.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A medieval monk may have been the first to recognize Halley's Comet as a repeating visitor, pushing back the known history of this famous cosmic traveler by centuries.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un moine médiéval aurait reconnu la comète de Halley des siècles avant Edmond Halley
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La comète de Halley est une comète célèbre qui revient près de la Terre environ tous les 76 ans. Elle porte le nom d'Edmond Halley, un astronome qui, au XVIIIe siècle, a montré que la comète observée à plusieurs dates était en fait la même revenant régulièrement.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – De nouvelles recherches montrent qu'un moine anglais médiéval nommé Eilmer de Malmesbury aurait remarqué les apparitions répétées de la comète près de 700 ans avant Halley. Eilmer a vu la comète en 989 puis en 1066, et il semblait comprendre qu'il s'agissait du même objet. Cette découverte vient d'anciens écrits du chroniqueur Guillaume de Malmesbury, étudiés par des chercheurs modernes.

**Impact** – Cette découverte modifie notre compréhension de l'histoire de l'astronomie. Elle suggère que les gens du Moyen Âge reconnaissaient le cycle de la comète bien avant la science moderne. Elle montre aussi comment les comètes étaient perçues à l'époque, souvent comme des signes d'événements importants ou effrayants, comme les guerres ou la mort des rois. L'histoire est même liée à une œuvre célèbre, la tapisserie de Bayeux, qui montre la comète en 1066.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient de continuer à étudier les archives historiques pour mieux comprendre les premières observations de comètes. Leur travail pourrait mener à de nouvelles idées sur la perception du ciel par les anciens et ouvrir un débat sur le nom de la comète.

**Phrase clé** – Un moine médiéval aurait été le premier à reconnaître la comète de Halley comme une visiteuse régulière, repoussant de plusieurs siècles l'histoire connue de ce célèbre voyageur cosmique.
articles.personality_title_es: Un monje medieval pudo haber reconocido el cometa Halley siglos antes que Edmond Halley
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El cometa Halley es un cometa famoso que se acerca a la Tierra aproximadamente cada 76 años. Recibe su nombre del astrónomo Edmond Halley, quien en el siglo XVIII demostró que varios avistamientos del cometa correspondían al mismo objeto que regresaba regularmente.

**Qué pasó** – Nuevas investigaciones revelan que un monje inglés medieval llamado Eilmer de Malmesbury pudo haber notado las apariciones repetidas del cometa casi 700 años antes que Halley. Eilmer vio el cometa en 989 y nuevamente en 1066, y parece que comprendió que era el mismo objeto. Este hallazgo proviene de escritos antiguos del historiador Guillermo de Malmesbury, estudiados por investigadores modernos.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento cambia nuestra comprensión de la historia de la astronomía. Sugiere que las personas en la Edad Media reconocían el patrón del cometa mucho antes que la ciencia moderna. También muestra cómo se veían los cometas en esa época, a menudo como señales de eventos importantes o temibles, como guerras o muertes de reyes. La historia está relacionada incluso con una obra famosa, el Tapiz de Bayeux, que muestra el cometa en 1066.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean seguir estudiando registros históricos para aprender más sobre las primeras observaciones de cometas. Su trabajo podría traer nuevas ideas sobre cómo las personas del pasado entendían el cielo y podría abrir debates sobre cambiar el nombre del cometa.

**Frase clave** – Un monje medieval pudo haber sido el primero en reconocer al cometa Halley como un visitante periódico, adelantando siglos la historia conocida de este famoso viajero cósmico.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed medieval-style painting of a radiant comet streaking across a starry night sky over an ancient European town, with a wise monk silhouette gazing thoughtfully upward from a stone monastery rooftop, while a historic tapestry-like border subtly weaves in comet motifs and celestial patterns symbolizing repeated comet appearances across centuries.
